Mechatronics Engineer · Melbourne, Australia

Henry Chiu

I design the safety-critical electronics that keep a student-built electric race car on the track — and the test rigs that prove hardware works before it ships. Master of Mechatronics, University of Melbourne, graduating 2026.

3 yrs FSAE electronics Electronics Team Lead 2025 Altium Designer Fusion 360 STM32
01 — JOURNEY

Three years, one car, real growth

Formula SAE teams design, build, and race an electric vehicle from scratch every year. I joined Melbourne University Racing's low-voltage electronics team in 2024 and grew from a first-time PCB designer into the section's team lead — then chose to go back to the bench to do my best technical work yet.

2024 · YEAR 1

First board, first lap — the hard way

I designed and manufactured the Master Shutdown System: the safety-critical PCB that monitors fault signals from across the car (insulation, battery management, braking plausibility, thermal overload) and opens the accumulator isolation relays to cut motor power the moment anything goes wrong. During car integration, one of my components began failing intermittently — only after full assembly, and only after repeated cycles. Under competition pressure I diagnosed the fault, reverted to a repaired earlier-generation board, and integrated it with the rest of the car.

FAULT: component failure during integration  →  RESOLVED: car ran its first lap in 7 years
2025 · YEAR 2

Electronics Team Lead

Led a team of five covering every sensor, loom, and PCB on the vehicle. Personally owned wire-harness design, parts and inventory management, and a revamp of the shutdown PCB. Under my lead, the team passed all inspections on schedule and competed in — and finished — every dynamic event.

ALL DYNAMIC EVENTS COMPLETED
2025 · INTERNSHIP

Clutch Industries — water dynamometer

Designed a full underwater thrust-testing rig in Fusion 360 to validate motor, propeller, and jet-pump configurations — from concept CAD through component specification, supplier procurement, and CNC coordination. The rig was subsequently assembled and commissioned. See the build ↓

2026 · YEAR 3

Back to the bench, by choice

Handed the lead role to a successor and returned to focused design work: STM32-based sensor nodes for accumulator temperature monitoring, and a redesigned, more compact shutdown system that carries every lesson from 2024 forward.

IN PROGRESS: final-year capstone — control electronics
02 — MUR / FSAE

Electronics for a 389 V race car

Melbourne University Racing competes in FSAE, building an electric race car from the ground up each season. My work sits in the low-voltage system: the boards and looms that sense, decide, and — when something is wrong — shut the whole car down safely.

FAULT DETECTED

2024: the board that broke slowly

My first shutdown board passed every bench test — then a component began failing intermittently after full car assembly, hidden among a dozen other integration issues.

FALLBACK DEPLOYED

Diagnose, revert, integrate

I isolated the failure, repaired a previous-generation board, and integrated it under deadline. The car passed scrutineering and ran its first competitive lap in seven years.

REDESIGNED

Lessons, latched in

The 2025 revamp finished every dynamic event. The 2026 revision is smaller, cleaner, and shaped by everything that board taught me about testing beyond the bench.

Try the shutdown logic

MSS · Interactive demo

A working model of the real latch behaviour. Trip any fault and the system latches it, opening the relays and killing the motor. Now try pressing RESET while the fault is still active — the latch ignores you. Clear the fault first, then reset. That asymmetry is the whole point of the design.

IMD
insulation monitor
BMS
battery mgmt
PDOC
thermal overload
ECU
controller fault
AIR RELAYS: CLOSED
MOTOR: RUNNING
SYSTEM NOMINAL — all channels clear.
03 — INTERNSHIP

From CAD model to commissioned rig

At Clutch Industries, I was tasked with designing a water dynamometer to validate the performance of different motor, propeller, and jet-pump configurations — part of the company's exploration into the water-sports market. I owned the full mechanical assembly in Fusion 360, specified components for the loads and underwater conditions they'd face, and ran procurement: liaising with suppliers including JLCMC and Alibaba vendors, and coordinating CNC machining to spec. By the end of my internship the design was complete and parts ordered; the rig was subsequently assembled and commissioned.

04 — SKILLS

What I bring to a team

ECAD & Hardware

  • Altium Designer — schematic capture & PCB layout
  • Safety-critical design — latching, fail-safe logic
  • Bench bring-up & test — power supplies, meters, debug
  • Fault-finding under pressure — proven at competition

Mechanical

  • Fusion 360 — assemblies, cross-sections, DFM
  • Component specification — loads, environments
  • Procurement — supplier liaison, JLCMC, Alibaba
  • CNC coordination — drawings to machined parts

Embedded

  • STM32 — custom sensor node design
  • Sensor interfacing — temperature monitoring
  • Signal conditioning — filtering, protection, references

Leading & Delivering

  • Team leadership — 5 engineers, full LV system
  • Wire-harness design — full-vehicle loom
  • Inventory & planning — parts, deadlines, scrutineering
  • Knowing when to hand over — and when to step up
05 — CONTACT

Open to graduate roles

Looking for graduate positions in electronics, embedded systems, or mechatronics — Melbourne-based, open to opportunity. If your team builds real hardware, I'd love to talk.
